channel.title: Bhattacharya Lab @ WashU
channel.description: Recent content on Bhattacharya Lab @ WashU

item.description:
Get in Touch

Our lab is soon to be up and running at WashU, and we are excited to build a team of curious, motivated scientists who want to ask overarching questions about arbovirus evolution and host-pathogen interactions. We are actively looking for enthusiastic people to join us at all career stages.

Prospective postdocs, graduate students and research staff should refer to our Research, Publications and Team pages to get to know our research interests and instructions on how to apply.

For a full and up-to-date list, refer to our Google Scholar profile.

2026

A conserved in-frame stop codon acts as a multipotent defense mechanism in alphaviruses. Bhattacharya T†, Freeman TS, Alleman EM, Wang F, Chechik L, Emerman M, Myles KM, Malik HS Science Advances, 2025 — Link † Corresponding author

2025

A conserved opal termination codon optimizes a temperature-dependent trade-off between protein production and processing in alphaviruses. Bhattacharya T†, Alleman EM, Freeman TS, Noyola AC, Emerman M, Malik HS Science Advances, 11(16):eads7933, 2025 — Link † Corresponding author

What We Study

Arboviruses encounter a unique evolutionary challenge: they must replicate in organisms from two distinct host phyla, arthropods and chordates, which diverged over 700 million years ago. Each host environment imposes competing selective pressures, including differences in temperature, immune responses, and dependency factors.

How do viruses overcome these challenges? Do different virus families converge on similar solutions? These questions motivate our research. We investigate viral evolution using functional genomics, integrating experimental and computational approaches to reveal the molecular strategies viruses employ to adapt to diverse host environments.

Lab Philosophy

“Somewhere, something incredible is waiting to be known.” – Carl Sagan

In our lab, we believe that pushing the boundaries of knowledge requires bold inquiry. We are committed to building a vibrant lab culture that is inclusive, collaborative, and supportive. We believe that good science requires a safe and supportive environment where everyone feels comfortable asking questions, making mistakes, and sharing ideas without fear of judgment.

Expectations

Everyone in the Lab

Principal Investigator

Tamanash Bhattacharya, Ph.D. (he/him)

Assistant Professor of Molecular Microbiology

Born and raised in Calcutta, Tamanash attended graduate school at Indiana University (Hoo Hoo Hoosiers!) in the labs of Richard Hardy and Irene Newton, studying how the bacterial endosymbiont Wolbachia pipientis, a staple member of arthropod microbiota, protects insects against RNA viruses. He then moved to Seattle to join Harmit Malik’s lab at Fred Hutch, where he set out to answer a fundamental question - how do alphaviruses maintain transmission across host species that diverged 700 million years ago? Using alphaviruses as a model system, Tamanash discovered viral strategies of overcoming temperature- and innate immunity- driven constraints. In his lab, he aims to understand universal principles and molecular “rules” of multi-host adaptation. Outside the lab, Tamanash enjoys spicy Chinese cuisine, tales of cosmic horror, and cherished times with family and friends.
